coredata.dc:title: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as the dual functional fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and pH detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier B.V. on behalf of King Saud University.
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Shenna
Hao, Yunping
Liu, Sha
Liu, Yanxu
Zhang, Ziyan
Fang, Mei
Geng, Lina
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots doped with boron and nitrogen atoms (B, N-CDs) were designed and prepared by hydrothermal strategy using citric acid, urea and borax as raw materials. The structure and optical properties of B, N-CDs were characterized by various characterization technologies. The results showed that the B, N-CDs exhibited good mono-dispersion, excellent fluorescence stability, and high fluorescence intensity (quantum yield up to 32 %). A dual-functional Fe3+ and pH detection platform based on B, N-CDs was constructed. The Fe3+ sensing method based on B, N-CDs fluorescence quenching showed an excellent linear relationship between 0.044–70 μM (R2 = 0.994), with a detection limit as low as 0.044 μM. Then, this detection method was successfully applied to the high-accuracy detection of Fe3+ in water samples and iron supplement tablets. The study of the quenching mechanism showed that the fluorescence of B, N-CDs was quenched by Fe3+ in two ways: static quenching and dynamic quenching. Moreover, B, N-CDs can also be used as a pH fluorescence sensor in the ranges of pH 2.0–4.8 and 5.2–7.6. The developed sensing system based on B, N-CDs has high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Fe3+, and a wide detection range for pH.
coredata.dcterms:subject: B, N-CDs
Fe3+
pH
Fluorescent probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical properties of the B, N-CDs
Detection mechanism of B, N-CDs for Fe3+

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM and size distribution (inset), (b) XRD pattern, and (c) FTIR spectrum of B, N-CDs. (d) XPS full survey (e) C 1 s, (f) N 1 s, (g) O 1 s and (h) B 1 s analysis results.
Spectral properties of B, N-CDs. (a) UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra. (b) Color coordinates and the photographed image under UV irradiation (inset). (c) Fluorescence emission spectra.

B, N-CDs were used for pH sensing. (a) The fluorescence intensity (bottom to top: pH 2–8), and the photographed image of B, N-CDs (inset) at different pH values. (b) The sigmoidal fitting plot for F/F0 versus different pH. The insets showed the linear calibration plots at 2.0–4.8 and 5.2–7.6. (c) The reversible repeat response of fluorescence intensity to pH change. (d) The UV–vis absorption spectra of B, N-CDs with increasing pH.

(a) Ion-selective study of B, N-CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectra of B, N-CDs with different concentrations of Fe3+. (c) The linear calibration plot for 1-F/F0 versus
C
Fe
3 +
(μM), F0 and F were the fluorescence intensity of B, N-CDs in the absence and the presence of Fe3+.

The UV–vis absorption spectra (a), zeta potential (b), fluorescence lifetime (c) of B, N-CDs and B, N-CDs-Fe3+. (d) Stern-Volmer plot for the quenching of B, N-CDs after adding Fe3+. (e) Fluorescence quenching mechanism diagram of Fe3+ reaction with B, N-CDs.
The recovery and precision of iron in the tablets.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: Peer review under responsibility of King Saud University. Production and hosting by Elsevier.

head.ce:title: Boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots as the dual functional fluorescent probe for Fe3+ and pH detection

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Hebei Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050024, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Material Science
Hebei Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Material Science, Hebei Key Laboratory of Organic Functional Molecules, Hebei Normal University, Shijiazhuang 050024, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this study.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots doped with boron and nitrogen atoms (B, N-CDs) were designed and prepared by hydrothermal strategy using citric acid, urea and borax as raw materials. The structure and optical properties of B, N-CDs were characterized by various characterization technologies. The results showed that the B, N-CDs exhibited good mono-dispersion, excellent fluorescence stability, and high fluorescence intensity (quantum yield up to 32 %). A dual-functional Fe3+ and pH detection platform based on B, N-CDs was constructed. The Fe3+ sensing method based on B, N-CDs fluorescence quenching showed an excellent linear relationship between 0.044–70 μM (R2 = 0.994), with a detection limit as low as 0.044 μM. Then, this detection method was successfully applied to the high-accuracy detection of Fe3+ in water samples and iron supplement tablets. The study of the quenching mechanism showed that the fluorescence of B, N-CDs was quenched by Fe3+ in two ways: static quenching and dynamic quenching. Moreover, B, N-CDs can also be used as a pH fluorescence sensor in the ranges of pH 2.0–4.8 and 5.2–7.6. The developed sensing system based on B, N-CDs has high selectivity and sensitivity for the detection of Fe3+, and a wide detection range for pH.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron and pH are critical to human health, the food industry, agricultural production and the environment [1–3]. The deficiency and excess of Fe3+ can cause the disorder of normal function of the human body [4,5]. In foods, the level of Fe3+ directly influences the color and odor, especially in drinks [6]. Stable pH conditions play an essential role in energy exchange, protein synthesis, enzyme activity, signal transduction pathway, and so on. Small changes in intracellular pH may lead to cellular dysfunction and many serious diseases [7]. Therefore, developing a selective and sensitive method for detecting Fe3+ and the pH level in biological systems and the environment is crucial.

Currently, various technologies and materials have been used to quantify Fe3+ or H+; among them, the fluorescence sensing methods based on carbon dots (CDs) have received widespread attention [8–10]. As a kind of fluorescence nanomaterial, CDs have the advantages of easy preparation and functionalization, good water solubility, excellent biocompatibility, and high fluorescent stability [11]. However, CDs are often not satisfactory in terms of selectivity, detection range and anti-interference. Yan et al. prepared CDs from hyperbranched polysiloxane by hydrothermal method to monitor Fe3+, but this detection method was interfered by Cu2+ and Hg2+ [12]. Shi et al. prepared CDs as Fe3+ probes with glucose, ammonia and phosphoric acid by hydrothermal method, but the selective experiment showed that the sensing method was strongly affected by Cu2+ [13]. Currently, many types of CDs-fluorescent probes for pH detection have been constructed, and these probes have displayed exemplary performance in pH sensing, but some of them have a narrow detection range. Gong et al. prepared CDs by treatment of acid fuchsin and measured pH values in the range of 5.0–7.4 [14]. Liu et al. synthesized CDs by methylcellulose, ethylenediamine and L-cysteine to detect pH values of 10 to 11.5 [15]. Guo et al. established a CDs-based pH visual detection method with a pH linear response region of 3.6 to 2.4 [10].

The functional groups on the surface of CDs play an essential role in the selectivity of metal ion fluorescent detection. In order to solve the above problems, heteroatomic doping was chosen. Designing and selecting appropriate heteroatoms was a practical approach to regulating the physicochemical and photochemical performance of CDs [16–18]. N-doping gives CDs a higher quantum yield (QY) due to the production of a new defect surface state, which increases electronic properties and improves radiative recombination [19]. N-CDs with N-containing groups can be applied to the measurement of metal ions through conjugation or reduction. In addition, boron-doped CDs have also been extensively investigated. Due to the low electronegativity of the B atoms, energy defects and active sites are induced, resulting in changes in the optical properties of CDs [20]. Wang group designed and synthesized boron and nitrogen co-doped carbon dots (B, N-CDs) to detect Fe3+, and the detection limit was 0.1 μM. They found that the boron atom of the boric acid group inclined to form sp3 hybrid B atom when combined with –OH of water, and three oxygen atoms of the boric acid group can coordinate with Fe3+, which improved the selectivity of Fe3+ detection [12]. Song group prepared B, N, S-CDs for detecting Fe3+ ions with high sensitivity and good selectivity, and the B, N, S-CDs were also used to detect pH from 1.60 to 7.00 [21]. Wang et al. prepared multi-doped CDs with 3-aminophenylboronic acid and measured pH values in the range of 3.0 to 9.0 [22]. Undoubtedly, these heteroatom-doped CDs fluorescent sensors significantly improve the performance of Fe3+ or H+ detection. However, there is still a lot of work to be done to improve the quantum yield (QY) and sensitivity and to develop nanoprobes for multifunctional sensing.

In our previous work, we reported the dual-functional CDs for detecting Hg2+ and ascorbic acid [23] and trifunctional CDs for mercury ion, glutathione and pH detection [24]. In this work, we expect to develop multifunctional CDs-based sensing methods by selecting raw materials to modulate the composition and surface functional groups of CDs. Herein, we developed a facile method for preparing B, N-CDs by hydrothermal treatment of citric acid, urea and borax. The morphology, elemental composition, functional groups and optical properties of B, N-CDs were characterized. The multifunctional fluorescence probe for Fe3+ and pH monitoring was established, and the sensing mechanism was studied. The prepared B, N-CDs have extensive potential for Fe3+ and pH monitoring in environmental and biological systems.

Citric acid, urea, sodium chloride and borax were obtained from Guangfu Chemical Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). Ferric chloride and quinine hydrochloride were purchased from Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). Other reagents were acquired from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd (Shanghai, China). All solutions were prepared with ultrafiltered water.

B, N-CDs were synthesized in combination with our previous work and methods reported in the literature, with minor modifications [23,25]. Citric acid (1.0521 g), urea (0.3003 g) and borax (0.19535 g) were dissolved in 15 mL deionized water and hydrothermal treated at 180 ℃ for 10 h. Then, the obtained dark green solution was centrifuged and dialyzed to remove any large particles and unreacted small molecules. Finally, the B, N-CDs solution was dried into powder at 85 ℃, dissolved into a solution (10 mg/mL), and stored at 4 ℃.

As a control, N-CDs without B doping were prepared. The preparation process referred to the above steps: 0.4 g of citric acid and 0.1250 g of urea were dissolved and reacted at 180 ℃ for 8 h. The following steps were the same as above.

The morphological characteristics of B, N-CDs were recorded by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, H-7650, Hitachi). X-ray diffraction (XRD) data were performed on an AXS-D8 X-ray diffractometer (Bruker). XPS data were carried out on a Thermo ESCALAB 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. Fourier transform infrared spectrum was collected on a Thermo SCIENTIFIC (Thermo). ZetaSizer Nano-ZS90 instrument (Malvern) was used to record the zeta potential. Agilent Cary60 spectrophotometer was applied for UV–vis absorption spectra.

Quinine sulfate was chosen as the reference substance for QY measurement. The QY of B, N-CDs was calculated as follows:
(1)
Yu = Y s ∙
Fu
Fs
∙
As
Au
In the above formula, Y was the quantum yield, and A was the absorbance; u and s refer to B, N-CDs and the quinine sulfate, respectively.

The photostability of B, N-CDs under ultraviolet irradiation (365 nm) was measured, and the fluorescence intensity was recorded every 5 minutes. The stability of B, N-CDs against ionic strength was investigated in NaCl solution, and the fluorescence intensities were measured when the concentrations of NaCl solutions were 0 to 0.30 mol/L. The pH stability was further studied and recorded by adjusting pH (2.0–––11.0) with Britton-Robison buffer solution.

100 μM of Mg2+, Pb2+, Ca2+, Cu2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, Hg2+, Ba2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Fe3+, Al3+, Na+ and Ag+ were added to the B, N-CDs solution (0.1 mg/mL), respectively. The fluorescence spectra were collected after 5 minutes of equilibrium.
Fe3+ with different concentrations (0–100 μM) was added into B, N-CDs solution to determine the fluorescence intensity.

The sensor was applied to the measurement of Fe3+ in tap water and lake water to verify its practicality. A series of standard concentrations of Fe3+ (0, 10, 30 and 50 μM) were added to the B, N-CDs. The fluorescence data were collected, and the mean and standard deviation were obtained by three independent experiments.

The iron supplement tablet (Labeled iron content: 60 mg tablet; Manufacturers: Shanxi Lijiu Pharmaceutical Co., LTD) was purchased from a pharmacy. The samples were pretreated with 1000 μL concentrated HNO3 and incubated for two hours. The pH of the solution was adjusted with NaOH solution (0.1 M). The concentration of Fe3+ in the tablet sample was measured. Subsequently, different concentrations of iron (10 and 25 μM) were added, and the recovery rate and RSD were calculated.

The morphology of B, N-CDs was revealed and measured through TEM (Fig. 1 a). B, N-CDs are uniform and monodispersed, with a particle size distribution of 3.5 to 8.5 nm and an average particle diameter of 5.5 nm. As displayed in Fig. 1 b, the XRD pattern of B, N-CDs has a broad peak around 24°, indicating that they were amorphous [26,27].

The functional groups and chemical composition of B, N-CDs were studied by FTIR spectrum (Fig. 1c). There were many characteristic peaks, including O–H/N–H [28] (3420 cm−1), C = O [29] (1580 cm−1) and C-N [30] (1436 cm−1). The other peaks at 1140 cm−1, 1085 cm−1 and 1040 cm−1 were assigned to the bending vibration of –OH in B-O–H, the C-B stretching vibration and the B-O–H bending vibration, respectively [21]. The above results indicated that B and N atoms were successfully doped into B, N-CDs.

The functional state and composition of B, N-CDs were explored in detail by XPS measurements. The full spectra (Fig. 1d) suggested that carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and boron were the major components. The high solution of C1s spectra at 284.8 eV, 286.55 eV and 288.25 eV were assigned to C = C/C–C, C-O/C-N and C = O/C = N [31]). Two peaks were displayed in the N1s spectra, corresponding to N–H at 399.9 eV and C-N/N-N at 401.35 eV [32]. The peaks presented in the O1s spectrum at 531.15 eV, 532.1 eV and 535.7 eV were C = O, C-O-C/C-OH and O − H [12], respectively. The peak at 192.35 eV in the B1s spectrum was the B-O bond. The results of FTIR and XPS analysis illustrated that the surface of prepared B, N-CDs was functionalized by various groups containing oxygen, nitrogen and boron, which may interact with metal/nonmetal ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of the B, N-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 2 a, B, N-CDs have two intense absorption peaks at 230 nm and 340 nm, which might be attributed to the π- π* transition of C = C and n- π* transition of C = O [33]. When the excitation wavelength was 360 nm, B, N-CDs showed the maximum fluorescence intensity at 450 nm. Moreover, a bright blue luminescence of solution was observed under UV irradiation (365 nm), as shown in the inset of Fig. 2b, which was consistent with the result in color coordinate. When the excitation wavelength changed from 330 nm to 380 nm (Fig. 2c), the maximum emission peak remained relatively constant (about 450 nm), and fluorescence intensity reached the maximum when excited at 360 nm. This excitation-independent fluorescence might be attributed to fewer surface defects and narrow particle size distribution [12 34]. Additionally, the QY of B, N-CDs was up to 32 %, which was superior to the recently prepared B, N-CDs in many references [12].

Then the stability of the B, N-CDs against ionic strengths, UV irradiation and pH change was studied. Fig. S1 showed that B, N-CDs have good salt resistance and UV resistance. B, N-CDs exhibited stable photoluminescence in alkaline systems and significant pH-dependent behavior in acid media, which illustrated that B, N-CDs may be helpful for pH sensing.

Fig. S1c showed that B, N-CDs exhibited excellent stability in alkaline solution, but the fluorescence intensity showed an increase with the pH value ranging from 2 to 8 (Fig. 3 a and d). Fig. 3a showed that the fluorescence intensity gradually increased with the pH range when excited at 365 nm. At the same time, the B, N-CDs solutions presented pale yellow under natural light (upper row), while the photoluminescence changed from light blue to dark blue, and the intensity increased significantly (lower row).

More importantly, the change in fluorescence intensity supplied an excellent chance to establish a pH measurement method. The ratio (F/F0) varied greatly under acidic pH, and the pKa value of B, N-CDs was 4.78 through the analysis and calculation of sigmoidal fitting. Two linear regression equations were obtained in the range of pH 2.0–––4.8 and 5.2–––7.6, and the results showed favorable linear relationships between fluorescence intensity and pH values. Therefore, a pH detection method based on the change of solution color and photoluminescence intensity was developed.

Furthermore, reversibility was quite essential for monitoring pH change under physiological conditions [35]. The pH of B, N-CDs solution was adjusted repeatedly between 7.0 and 2.0, and the fluorescence intensities were measured, respectively. The fluorescence almost recovered successfully during the repeated cycles (Fig. 3c), suggesting that B, N-CDs possessed excellent reversibility in the pH of 2.0–––8.0 and can be used to monitor pH at acidic conditions.

It was worth noting that when pH was in the range of 2.0 to 4.0, the fluorescence emission peak first exhibited a blue shift. As pH increased from 5.0 to 8.0, the peak position remained the same, as shown in Fig. 3a. Correspondingly, with the increase of pH value, the absorption peak blue-shifted first and then red-shifted, the absorption intensity gradually increased, as shown in Fig. 3d. These two-stage changes of peak position in fluorescence spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra may be due to the changes of electron state on the B, N-CDs surface. The change rules and nodes of absorption peak and emission peak were closely connected to the pKa of B, N-CDs in Fig. 3b. Combined with the above results, the pH response of B, N-CDs may be related to the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl and amino functional groups. Moreover, the explanation for protonation and deprotonation between C(=OH+)NH3 + and C(=O)NH2 was also persuasive [36].

The response of B, N-CDs to various metal ions is shown in Fig. 4 a. Fe3+ caused almost entirely fluorescence quenching, but the fluorescence change was negligible when other metal ions were added. For comparison, N-CDs without B atoms were prepared and their selectivity was investigated (Fig. S2). The result showed that no considerable fluorescence quenching effect on N-CDs was observed by various metal ions containing Fe3+. Through contrast and analysis, it was found that B, N-CDs doped with B atoms have a higher selectivity for Fe3+. The possible reason was the functional groups of B, N-CDs. The B atom in the boric acid groups tends to combine with the hydroxyl group in water and form a sp3 hybrid B atom [12]. Therefore, the presence of sp3 hybrid B atom on the B, N-CDs surface introduced more hydroxyl groups, which were suitably coordinated for binding to Fe3+. Consequently, B, N-CDs exhibited higher selectivity for Fe3+ than N-CDs. The quenching mechanisms will be further discussed below.

Subsequently, the quantitative determination of Fe3+ by B, N-CDs was performed. Upon adding Fe3+ (0 μM − 90 μM) to B, N-CDs solution, the fluorescence intensity decreased gradually (Fig. 4b). And Fig. 4c exhibited that the quenching coefficient (1-F/F0) versus the concentration of Fe3+ (
C
Fe
3 +
) showed good linearity with a corresponding regression coefficient square (R2) of 0.994. The correlation equation was 1-F/F0 = -0.01 + 0.0115
C
Fe
3 +
. And the LOD calculated by the 3σ/S method was 0.044 μM. The detection performances of different fluorescent CDs on Fe3+ were presented in Table S1. All the above results substantially demonstrated that B, N-CDs would be a highly sensitive fluorescence probe for Fe3+.

The established fluorescence probe based on B, N-CDs was applied to the natural environment system and its performance was evaluated. The sensitivity of our sensor meets the requirement for Fe3+ measurement in drinking water quality criterion (5.36 μM) set by the Ministry of Health P. R. China [37]. So, the content of Fe3+ in the tap and lake water samples can be measured by the spiked recovery method. Table S2 presented that the recovery for tap water was 97.7–102 %, with a relative standard deviation (RSD) ranging from 0.84 % to 1.52 %. For lake water, the recoveries ranged from 98 % to 100.4 %, and the RSD was less than 1.52 %. In addition, the iron content in the tablet was determined. The sample was first nitrated to release the Fe3+ from the tablets. The RSD in different samples was 1.10–2.40 %, and the recovery rate was 92.10 %-100.7 % (Table 1 ). All the results indicated that the established sensing method was reliable and feasible in actual sample detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection mechanism of B, N-CDs for Fe3+
ce:section.ce:para: In general, the fluorescence quenching process may be caused by electron transfer (ET), inner filter effect (IFE), fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET), dynamic quenching or static quenching [38]. Fig. 5 a shows that the absorbance peak of B, N-CDs at 340 nm decreased obviously after adding Fe3+. Because the ET and IFE processes do not influence the absorption spectrum, ET and IFE mechanisms were excluded [34]. The FRET mechanism can be ruled out because there was no overlap between the absorption of Fe3+ and the emission of B, N-CDs. Moreover, Fig. 5b showed that the fluorescence lifetime of B, N-CDs changed from 7.23 ns to 7.43 ns before and after adding Fe3+. It indicated that it was not FRET or a single dynamic quenching mechanism [39]. Therefore, the most likely explanation was static quenching, that was, the formation of a stable non-fluorescent complex between B, N-CDs and Fe3+ ion. In summary, the quenching process involves static quenching, which has the following properties: (1) changes in the absorption spectrum of the B, N-CDs indicated the formation of new substance (Fig. 5a), (2)The fluorescence lifetime displayed no significant change in the presence of Fe3+ (Fig. 5b), (3)The zeta potential of B, N-CDs was −21.5 mV because of the abundant –COOH and –OH exposure on its surface. Subsequently, the zeta potential of B, N-CDs-Fe3+ increased to 6.34 mV (Fig. 5c).

To further understand the fluorescence quenching mechanism, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to analyze. Dynamic quenching and static quenching are described as equations (2) and (3) [12], respectively.
(2)
F 0
/
F = 1 +
K
s v
[ Q ]
(3)
F 0
/
F = 1 + K
[ Q ]
Where [Q] is the concentration of Fe3+. Ksv is the Stern-Volmer quenching constant, and K is the formation constant of B, N-CDs and Fe3+.

The Stern-Volmer plot obtained in our experiment in Fig. 5d was an upward curve concave toward the Y-axis, which revealed that the fluorescence quenching was not only static quenching but also dynamic quenching [40,41]. The most probably quenching mechanism can be explained as follows (Fig. 5e.): When the –OH of B, N-CDs coordinated with Fe3+ to form a non-fluorescent complex, the zeta potential increased. The Fe3+ in the solution would also collide with B, N-CDs. The fluorescence of B, N-CDs was eventually quenched by dynamic and static quenching.

In summary, we successfully designed and synthesized B, N-CDs using citric acid, urea and borax via the hydrothermal method. The B, N-CDs with an average size of 5.5 nm emitted blue fluorescence, and the QY was up to 32 %. A high selectivity and sensitivity Fe3+ fluorescence quenching sensor was developed. The sensing method showed an excellent linear relationship between 0.044–70 μM (R2 = 0.994). Then, this analytical method has been successfully used to detect Fe3+ in water samples and iron supplement tablets with high accuracy. Finally, the detection mechanism was investigated, including the static quenching mechanism and dynamic quenching mechanism. Besides, the B, N-CDs were used to measure pH in the range of pH 2.0–4.8 and 5.2–7.6 due to proton transfer. The proposed Fe3+ detection assay based on B, N-CDs solved the problem of unsatisfying selectivity of CDs well, and the pH sensing method showed a wide detection range. We hope that this work can provide ideas and inspiration for improving the properties and application of heteroatom-doped CDs.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by grants from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (31201305), the Natural Science Foundation of Hebei Province (B2019205054, E2022205011, B2021205001), the Teaching Reform Project of Hebei Normal University (2021XJJG047), and the Science Foundation of Hebei Normal University (L2023J01).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jscs.2023.101775.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

Nano Res.

J. Colloid Interface Sci.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Solid sensory polymer substrates for the quantification of iron in blood, wine and water by a scalable RGB technique
